sections.markdown:
- 5 km easy run
- Only take this if Tuesday's reset day leaves the right ankle calmer rather than merely tolerable.
- Keep the effort fully conversational and shorten or stop early if the peroneal tendon starts talking again.
- The goal is to keep a little frequency without breaking the reset-day pattern.

- This was planned as a `5 km` easy ankle-check run after the Tuesday reset day, and the completed run deliberately came in much shorter at `2.156 km` in `14:55` moving and elapsed time. That missed the distance prescription by `2.844 km`, but it matched the more important instruction in the note: keep the right peroneal tendon quiet and stop early rather than force volume.
- Strava and Apple Health identify the same session (`18303333870` / `7E9AC687-19DC-41C2-8FA0-52975F207386`). The cost stayed genuinely low, with Strava HR at `132.3 bpm` average and `146 bpm` max, Apple Health at `131.6 bpm` average and `146 bpm` max, no recorded climbing, and no stoppage in the stream.

- The short-term win was restraint. This run kept a little race-week frequency without turning Wednesday into another compromised fitness session, which made the Thursday reset day and Friday check-in run more defensible.
- Because the second half quickened from an estimated `7:36 /km` to `6:12 /km` while HR rose from `130.0` to `141.0 bpm`, the next session still needed to stay short and honest rather than chase the missed `5 km`.

- Long term, this supports the half-marathon and marathon build more by proving the ankle-management rule can actually override the calendar. The useful habit here is stopping with the tendon still manageable, not banking every planned kilometre.
- That matters for the Parramatta and Sydney goals because the later block will only tolerate higher volume if easy and injured-tendon days remain cheap enough to repeat.

- Strava moving pace averaged `6:55 /km` across `2.156 km` in `14:55`.
- The first half averaged an estimated `7:36 /km`; the second half averaged `6:12 /km`, so the run finished quicker even though the total session stayed very short.

- Strava HR was `132.3 bpm` average and `146 bpm` max; Apple Health was nearly identical at `131.6 bpm` average and `146 bpm` max.
- Distance-split HR rose from `130.0 bpm` in the first half to `141.0 bpm` in the second, which is a mild rise but still comfortably below the harder aerobic bands used elsewhere in this block.

- Moving time and elapsed time were both `14:55`, so there was no meaningful stop cost.
- The stream shows `0` stop segments and `0.0%` stationary samples, which makes this a clean short outing rather than a broken attempt to complete the full prescription.

- Strava recorded `0.0 m` of climbing, so terrain did not materially affect the read.
- With the route flat and the duration short, the main training signal is the deliberately reduced load rather than any aerobic stimulus.
